raw_case:
Fulminant amoebic colitis: a challenging diagnosis for the surgeon


Abstract

Fulminant amoebic colitis is a rare complication of amoebiasis that carries a high mortality rate. Its diagnosis is challenging and requires a high index of suspicion, and its early recognition is a priority to provide timely medical and surgical treatment. We present the case of a male patient who came to the emergency department with unspecific clinical presentation of abdominal pain, systemic inflammatory response and imaging study showing intestinal perforation of the right colon. Fecal peritonitis and perforation at the level of the hepatic flexure were observed, so a right hemicolectomy with terminal ileostomy was performed. Despite adequate medical and surgical treatment, the patient presented progressive deterioration and died. Colon perforation due to Entamoeba histolytica was the final diagnosis.

Introduction

Amoebiasis is a parasitic infection caused by Entamoeba histolytica (E. hystolytica). It is endemic to some tropical regions of the world and is considered the second leading cause of death from parasitic diseases worldwide [1].The main route of transmission is the ingestion of food contaminated with cysts. Poor hygiene conditions, tropical climates, overcrowding, inadequate fecal waste management, malnutrition, alcoholism, and recent travel to endemic areas are considered risk factors favoring transmission [2].Most cases are asymptomatic; however, 10%–20% develop amoebic colitis and liver abscesses. Amebiasis can involve any part of the intestine but mainly affects the cecum and ascending colon [3]. Its clinical presentation in case of bowel involvement represents a spectrum, including asymptomatic cases, dysentery cases, and, in extreme cases, fulminant amoebic colitis with peritonitis (FAC) [4, 5].

Case report

We present the case of a 44-year-old man with no history of chronic diseases who was admitted to the emergency department with a one-week history of diffuse abdominal pain, predominantly in the right hypochondrium, and fever. Physical examination showed tachycardia (144 beats per minute), blood pressure 120/86 mm/Hg, generalized abdominal pain on palpation without peritoneal signs, and a palpable mass in the right upper quadrant. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis (17 000/mm3), neutrophilia (82%), severe anemia (hemoglobin levels of 6.8 g/dl), platelet count 148 000/mm3, serum creatinine 0.7 mg/dl, moderate hyponatremia (128 mmol/L), and hypoalbuminemia (1.6 g/dl). A contrasted abdominal computed tomography was requested as part of his approach, showing thickening of the right colon wall and perforation of the colon wall at the level of the hepatic flexure with communication to a subphrenic abscess with segmental destruction of the right hepatic lobe parenchyma and diffusely distributed hepatic abscesses (Fig. 1).The patient underwent surgery and intraoperative findings included free intestinal fluid in the abdominal cavity, perforation of the colon at the hepatic angle, and a right subphrenic abscess in continuity with the right hepatic lobe. Abdominal cavity lavage and a right hemicolectomy with terminal ileostomy were performed. Histopathological analysis confirmed the diagnosis of acute colitis with infiltration of E. histolytica trophozoites (Fig. 2).As part of the treatment, broad-spectrum antibiotic was started since the admission of the patient, including coverage for gram-negative microorganisms with metronidazole, which is part of the antiamoebic treatment; however, in the postoperative period, the patient developed refractory septic shock that culminated in the patient's death.

Discussion

Acute amoebic colitis presents with diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and blood in the stool. The most feared complication of amoebic dysentery is acute FAC, occurring in 0.5% of cases and carrying mortality of 55% to 88% [6].Risk factors for developing complicated amoebic infection have been described, including age over 60 years, limited access to potable water, use of corticosteroid therapy, chronic kidney disease and diabetes mellitus [7], concomitant liver abscess, signs of peritonitis, leukocytosis, hyponatremia, hypokalemia and hypoalbuminemia [3]. Our patient lived in an endemic area and had a concomitant liver abscess, leukocytosis, hyponatremia, and hypoalbuminemia as risk factors for the development of this entity.Serological tests and stool studies can be performed for diagnosis. Cyst visualization can be performed under microscopy, although with low sensitivity (60%). Stool real-time polymerase chain reaction (sensitivity 79% and specificity 96%), stool antigen detection with enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (sensitivity 55%–100% and specificity 93%–100%) [8, 9], detection of antibodies by serologic assay (indirect fluorescent, counter immunoelectrophoresis or enzyme linked immunosorbent assay) with a sensitivity of 60% to 90% [10] can also be used. No serological tests or stool tests were performed initially on our patient because his clinical presentation required treatment with an emergency surgery, and the diagnosis was confirmed after histopathological analysis of the surgical specimen.Computed tomography is considered the study of choice in the evaluation of this complication, although the imaging findings tend to be nonspecific as they overlap with those of other conditions, the findings include thickening of the colon wall, intestinal pneumatosis and pneumoperitoneum in case of intestinal perforation [7].The management of invasive E. histolytica infections includes systemic treatment with drugs such as metronidazole, nitazoxanide y secnidazole [8]. Since admission to the emergency department, our patient was started on broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy with coverage for gran-negative microorganisms, including metronidazole, due to findings in imaging studies suggestive of intestinal perforation.The diagnosis of this condition is challenging, and histological analysis will have to make differential diagnosis with entities such as intestinal tuberculosis, malignant tumors, and inflammatory bowel disease, being the presence of trophozoites loaded with red blood cells in the thickness of the tissues pathognomonic of an invasive disease [11].In addition to supportive care and antibiotic therapy, surgical management is a cornerstone in the treatment of FAC, preferring complete resection of the affected segment over conservative surgical treatment due to the risk of suture dehiscence [3].

Conclusions

Fulminant amoebic colitis is a rare entity whose clinical relevance lies in its high mortality rate related to misdiagnosis. Considering this disease as part of the differential diagnosis in endemic areas and when the patient refers to a history of recent travel to endemic areas increases the diagnostic suspicion, early recognition, and treatment. Surgical management with resection of the affected segment should be considered in case of signs of peritonitis and imaging findings suggesting bowel perforation.

Conflict of interest statement

The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

Funding

None declared.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 44-year-old male
- Chief Complaint: Diffuse abdominal pain, predominantly in the right hypochondrium, and fever for one week
- History of Present Illness: One-week history of diffuse abdominal pain localized to the right hypochondrium with fever.
- Past Medical History: None
- Personal History: Resides in an endemic area for Entamoeba histolytica
- Family History: None
- Physical Examination: Tachycardia (144 bpm), blood pressure 120/86 mmHg, generalized abdominal tenderness without peritoneal signs, and a palpable mass in the right upper quadrant.
- Ancillary Tests: Laboratory findings: leukocytosis (17,000/mm³), neutrophilia (82%), severe anemia (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL), platelet count 148,000/mm³, serum creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, hyponatremia (128 mmol/L), and hypoalbuminemia (1.6 g/dL). CT abdomen: thickened right colon, hepatic flexure perforation, subphrenic abscess, hepatic parenchymal destruction, diffuse liver abscesses. Histopathology: Acute colitis with Entamoeba histolytica trophozoite infiltration.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Presentation and Imaging**: The patient's fever, abdominal pain, leukocytosis, and CT findings of hepatic abscesses and colon perforation suggest an intra-abdominal infection. Differential includes:
   - Amoebic colitis (due to residing in an endemic area, imaging findings of hepatic abscess, and colonic perforation).
   - *Diverticulitis*: Less likely, as the hepatic flexure location and liver abscesses are atypical.
   - *Malignancy*: Unlikely given the acute presentation and trophozoites found on histology.
   - *Inflammatory Bowel Disease*: Unlikely, as the patient presented acutely and had no prior chronic symptoms.
   - *Bacterial peritonitis or gastrointestinal perforation*: These are considered, but imaging and subsequent histopathology confirmed an amoebic etiology.

2. **Histopathology Confirmation**: The confirmed presence of Entamoeba histolytica trophozoites in the tissue was diagnostic, ruling out bacterial or other causes of colitis.

3. **Other Work-Up Limitations**: Serological or stool tests for amoebiasis were not performed due to the emergent surgical requirement, but the invasive colitis findings and endemic exposure were consistent with fulminant amoebic colitis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Fulminant amoebic colitis with bowel perforation and peritonitis secondary to invasive Entamoeba histolytica.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Fulminant amoebic colitis secondary to Entamoeba histolytica.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's try to work through this case step by step. The patient is a 44-year-old male with a week of abdominal pain, mainly in the right hypochondrium, and fever. Let me start by listing the key information.

First, the chief complaint is right upper quadrant abdominal pain and fever. The location of the pain is important. The right hypochondrium area includes organs like the liver, gallbladder, and parts of the intestine. So possible issues could be hepatitis, cholecystitis, or maybe something intestinal.

He's from an area endemic for Entamoeba histolytica. That's a big clue. Amoebiasis is common in such regions. So I should keep amoebic infections in mind. Amoebic dysentery, liver abscesses are possible here.

Looking at the physical exam: tachycardia (144 is pretty high), normal BP, abdominal tenderness but no peritoneal signs. A palpable mass in the right upper quadrant. Peritoneal signs like rigidity or rebound tenderness would suggest peritonitis, but their absence might mean it's contained. The mass could be an abscess or enlarged organ.

Ancillary tests: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia suggests bacterial infection. Severe anemia (Hb 6.8) is concerning. Possible causes could be chronic disease, blood loss, or maybe hemolysis. Platelets are slightly low (148k), but not critically. Hyponatremia and hypoalbuminemia – hypoalbuminemia might be due to liver issues or malnutrition, or maybe loss through the gut. Hyponatremia could be due to dehydration or SIADH from infection.

CT findings: Thickened right colon, perforation at hepatic flexure, subphrenic abscess, liver destruction, multiple liver abscesses. Histopathology shows E. histolytica trophozoites in acute colitis. Wait, the histopathology already found the amoeba. So that's definitive for amoebic colitis. But the liver abscesses would be a complication of that. So the progression could be from intestinal infection to liver involvement via the portal system.

So putting it all together: The patient has a history from an endemic area. The CT shows colon perforation and liver abscesses. The histopathology confirms E. histolytica. The complications here are the perforation leading to subphrenic abscess, and multiple liver abscesses. The anemia might be due to chronic infection or possibly bleeding from the colonic ulcers. Hyponatremia and hypoalbuminemia are common in severe infections and malnutrition.

So the primary diagnosis is amoebic colitis complicated by liver abscesses and colonic perforation. The presence of trophozoites in the histology confirms the amoebic infection. The liver abscesses are a typical extraintestinal manifestation of E. histolytica. The perforation is a severe complication of the colitis, leading to the subphrenic abscess. The fever, leukocytosis, and neutrophilia all align with an infectious process. The right upper quadrant mass could be the liver abscess or the inflamed colon.

Therefore, the final diagnosis should be amoebic colitis with liver abscesses and complications like perforation and subphrenic abscess.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s residence in an **Entamoeba histolytica-endemic area**, combined with **diffuse abdominal pain, fever, and right hypochondrium tenderness**, raises suspicion for amoebic infection. Amoebic colitis and liver abscesses are common complications of invasive amoebiasis.

<step 2> **CT findings** (hepatic flexure perforation, liver abscesses, subphrenic abscess) and **histopathology confirming E. histolytica trophozoites** directly link the clinical presentation to invasive amoebic disease. Liver abscesses typically result from hematogenous spread from colonic infection.

<step 3> **Severe anemia and hypoalbuminemia** align with chronic inflammation and protein loss from colonic mucosal damage. **Leukocytosis and neutrophilia** reflect systemic infection, while **hyponatremia** may stem from sepsis or fluid shifts.

<step 4> The absence of peritoneal signs despite perforation suggests localized containment by surrounding structures. The **palpable right upper quadrant mass** likely corresponds to the hepatic abscesses or inflamed colon.

### Answer:
Amoebic colitis with hepatic abscesses complicating perforation and subphrenic abscess.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s residence in an **Entamoeba histolytica-endemic area**, combined with **diffuse abdominal pain, fever, and right hypochondrium tenderness**, raises suspicion for amoebic infection. Amoebic colitis and liver abscesses are common complications of invasive amoebiasis.

**CT findings** (hepatic flexure perforation, liver abscesses, subphrenic abscess) and **histopathology confirming E. histolytica trophozoites** directly link the clinical presentation to invasive amoebic disease. Liver abscesses typically result from hematogenous spread from colonic infection.

**Severe anemia and hypoalbuminemia** align with chronic inflammation and protein loss from colonic mucosal damage. **Leukocytosis and neutrophilia** reflect systemic infection, while **hyponatremia** may stem from sepsis or fluid shifts.

The absence of peritoneal signs despite perforation suggests localized containment by surrounding structures. The **palpable right upper quadrant mass** likely corresponds to the hepatic abscesses or inflamed colon.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: The patient presents with fever, abdominal pain, leukocytosis, and CT findings of hepatic abscesses and colon perforation, suggesting an intra-abdominal infection. The differential diagnoses include amoebic colitis, diverticulitis, malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, and bacterial peritonitis or gastrointestinal perforation.

Step 2: Considering the patient's residence in an endemic area, imaging findings of hepatic abscesses and colonic perforation, and the presence of Entamoeba histolytica trophozoites on histology, amoebic colitis is the most likely diagnosis. Diverticulitis, malignancy, and inflammatory bowel disease are less likely due to atypical locations and the acute presentation without prior chronic symptoms.
Step 3: Histopathology confirms the presence of Entamoeba histolytica trophozoites, ruling out other causes such as bacteria or other organisms.
Step 4: The final diagnosis is fulminant amoebic colitis with bowel perforation and peritonitis secondary to invasive Entamoeba histolytica.
